content:
We place the horizontal line A as the x–axis, and let circle B have center \((0,R)\) with \(R=2008\).  Thus the line has “curvature” \(k_0=0\) and circle B has curvature \(k_B=1/R\).  We build a chain of circles \(C_1,C_2,\dots\), each tangent to the x–axis, to \(B\), and to its predecessor.

By the Soddy (Descartes)‐circle theorem, if a new circle \(C_n\) of curvature \(k_n\) is tangent to three given mutually tangent “circles” of curvatures \(k_0,k_B,k_{n-1}\), then
\[k_n \;=\;k_0+k_B+k_{n-1}\;\pm\;2\sqrt{k_0k_B+k_Bk_{n-1}+k_{n-1}k_0}.\]
Here \(k_0=0\), so
\[k_n \;=\;k_B+k_{n-1}\pm2\sqrt{k_B\,k_{n-1}}.\]
The choice “\(-\) ” gives the larger radius (smaller curvature) that indeed makes \(C_2\) larger than \(C_1\).  Writing
\(\sqrt{k_n}=\sqrt{k_{n-1}}-\sqrt{k_B},\)
and since \(k_1=1\) (so \(\sqrt{k_1}=1\)) and \(\sqrt{k_B}=1/\sqrt{R}\), one finds by induction
\[
\sqrt{k_n}
\;=\;
1-\frac{n-1}{\sqrt{R}}.
\]
Hence
\[
k_n
=\Bigl(1-\frac{n-1}{\sqrt{R}}\Bigr)^2,
\quad
r_n=\frac1{k_n}
=\frac1{\bigl(1-\tfrac{n-1}{\sqrt{R}}\bigr)^2}.
\]
Geometric circles exist (with positive finite radius) exactly so long as
\[
1-\frac{n-1}{\sqrt{R}}>0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
n-1<\sqrt{R}.
\]
Here \(\sqrt{2008}\approx44.82\), so \(n-1<44.82\) means \(n\le45\).  Thus the largest \(n\) for which all circles lie to the right of \(P\) and are genuine circles is

\boxed{45}.
